item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Image is owned by Tim at Westwood Lodge Ilkley Moor Cottages, not under an explicit Creative Commons license. As such, it is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0 and has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd been imagining "thirty" isn't the upper limit, just how many have been previously noted at the same time. There is no genuine upper limit. Sure a single MTF trooper can kill hundreds of the Grandmother Horde but there are potentially billions of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with many of the other posters. The Containment efforts seem misguided. The article does not make clear that there is a limited number of manifestations, and so the extreme efforts to contain the targets seem without benefit. The lack of attempts to contain the manifestations collectively or individually, extreme efforts to limit control and contain the target, and the willingness to work with the anomaly to the point of providing supplies, all paint a picture where the Foundation is assisting the anomaly rather than containing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How can u monsters do this to these sweet old ladies? They are grandmas! :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it is heavily implied, but without being spelled out (and especially with the time frames being so ambiguous) the Foundation here look slightly sociopathic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP Foundation—Turning Innocent, Loving Grannies into Creepy Anomalies since 1911
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this. Harmless in itself, even bordering on the ridiculous, yet creepy at the same time, with the Foundation reaction as cold and inhumane as ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know that 'nonna' is Italian for granny - but not much else! Maybe twenty words total. Just something I've picked up from my friend whose grandmother is Italian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
nonna

Do you know italian?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Horrifying, but what's with those nicknames for grandmothers? Gramgram? Surely not? Maybe an American thing, I guess - and I suppose this is confined to that country, as there's no 'granny' or 'nan' or 'Oma' or 'nonna'…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I added a picture. hope it's acceptable!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought "being trapped in a house by creepy old people chasing you" was similar enough to not up vote, combined with a few other points, but I respect your opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see much comparison to the Doctor Who version. Yes, creepy old people. But having aliens inside you that turn people to dust is a fair bit different from kindly pseudogrannys with slight sociopathic tendencies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love the concept. VERY unique!

But I'm with the crowd on the over-the-top containment procedures. SCP stands for Secure, Contain, PROTECT. You're not exactly protecting the victims of this thing if you lobotomize them! Lobotomizing a D-Class I can understand, or an innocent if it means containing a Keter class, but not a harmless, overly-friendly SCP like this. In all honesty, I think most people wouldn't mind an SCP like this around their own house.

Still, it is the cold Foundation and I understand where you're coming from, but I think the most they would do is imprison someone to contain something like this… not mutilate them or break their personhood.

This irks me so no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, it's mediocre, but I'll upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept, but the containment procedures just seem over the top to me. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because then they appear somewhere else

Ah. Then the article should make it clear that only one appears at a time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to neutral vote this due to the mildly creepy concept, but then it just dragged on with what I expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree that the containment procedures are kinda off. The Foundation should be able to keep someone in their house without physical restraints, and even be able to allow them a decent amount of freedom within the house. I mean, a luxury prison is still a prison, but we don't need to go for a tiny cage here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I'm crazy but this doesn't seem that deadly or bad.

A bunch of grandmas taking care of someone. Granted, it's a prison like environment but at least the company is good!

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Without it being clear that 1911 targets only 1 person at a time (like there won't be grannies in both Chicago and New York), this makes the Foundation seem awfully callous. I mean, I get that 1911 needs to be contained, but why are we not capturing them and attempting to get them to latch onto a D like we normally do in situations like this?

That aspect right now bugs me in a serious way. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
grammatorial combat

"You call that fighting, private? My GRANDMAS can be here in her 28 WHEELCHAIRS and she will whip the SHIT out of ALL of your asses and hand them to you on COOKIE TRAYS!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because then they appear somewhere else, and the Foundation is engaged in an inordinately expensive game of whack-a-mole wherein they have to monitor for signs of anything that could be a 1911 infestation, identify its target, dispatch a team to engage in grammatorial combat, erase the memories of witnesses and the target (which could very quickly exceed several dozen), then do it all over again. That's not containment by any measure. That's an utter failure to establish any, instead responding reactively to manifestations. While 1911 maintains contact with a target, it doesn't relocate, and none of the above is necessary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't really enjoy this. Possibly because of a long day, or possibly because Father Ted and Doctor Who have already done similar things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless the grandmas, when left alone, draw way too much attention to themselves, there's no reason to keep the guy imprisoned in his own home (where, as the article notes, it's hard to keep him inside without restraining him indefinitely) instead of just kidnapping him and keeping him somewhere in a cell (or, heck, tricking him into going on a lengthy vacation somewhere) until the grannies vanish.
It's either
- keep him imprisoned and possibly restrained for decades until he dies
- or keep him in a cell for a finite time until the grandmas die.
The current procedures are sadistic for no real good reason.

Say — now that I think about it, why won't the Foundation target the grandmas themselves? Once infestation occurs, just send in men who will overpower and take away the "escaped elderly dementia patients" — they're just harmless old women, they don't exhibit super strength, they don't indefinitely keep teleporting back. So there's even less reason to harass the victim.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very much improved over the version I saw. The interview was my upvote moment. That being said, you could probably uncollapse everything collapsed here and it would be improved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad this actually became an article. Good job man!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two quick points I'd like to make

1. I'm not sure if this was meant to seem funny or not, but I found it quite entertaining. +1 for this article.

2. During SCP-1911's last line of the interview (Addendum SCP-1911-T), she says, "I was so sad to see him go." Not sure if this bugs anyone else, but it slightly bothers me. It would make more sense to me if it read, "It was so sad to see him go." It's no big problem in my opinion, just me being knit-pickey!

Anywho! This is a nice article overall. Definitely one of my new personal favourites!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do you want to risk breaking containment? Do you hate America? Is that it? Do you hate freedom?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would the Foundation push that to find out? They'd lose containment. If they knew for sure—or even a rough estimate—it would be different. But they're not about to lose containment when the information they have is functional.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Be more ethical? Sure.

Make more sense? No, because that messes up the containment, and that's the whole purpose here. We contain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this. One question:

SCP-1911 continues to occupy the residence until the target expires or leaves for an extended period of time

Hate to requote what was quoted above, but the salient point here is, just how long is that? A variable length of time? Is there a minimum?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't really understand the containment procedures.

In all instances, SCP-1911 continues to occupy the residence until the target expires or leaves for an extended period of time

So wouldn't it make more sense (and be more ethical) to simply abduct and imprison the target for some time, then give them amnesiacs, instead of re-enacting Sloth's fate from Se7en with them?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the list of assorted "grandma"-related diminutives ought to have each item in quoties.

That said, creepy as hell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If this isn't the highest rated thing on the site inside of a month, I'm gonna be sorely disappointed.
Bang-up job, Troy. Bang-up job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god, it's Nanageddon!

And yet, it still creeps me out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What i like most about this is that it SEEMS to be harmless, then you realize it's the world's kindest prison. The kindness kills.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He upvoted it. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Burns stahp that critique is vague and unhelpful

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is bad and you should feel bad

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ack! Thanks, mate. Missed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Edit: Never mind, Observer fixed it. Overall I like the concept here, and seeing how people reacted to the SCP was an interesting read. +1.

Edit edit: I find the collapsibles irritating, I didn't see that very last addendum because of them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I saw a "Dr.[][][][][]" in there and changed it to a blackbox. Hope that was okay. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You need to give them more Lemon Pledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If anyone can find a creepy hoard of old ladies to make the picture, then feel free to add it.

SCP was inspired by Gargus in Site19.

Enjoy!
